to ask,
A lot of companies would hire me because they are trying to diversify their channels and be less dependent on just paid advertising or at least just paid search in particular.
And so I've had a long career that it's gone me under different ways from agency side to in-house, but largely that's why a lot of brands would hire me.
And it's very relevant to like why the average customer hires us.

You know, me being a customer obviously is going to have some of that connection, but goal today is just to walk you through a little bit of like the mindset of our customers, how that translates to some of the use cases we cover and, you know, clarifies just a few points that I find a lot of folks joining may be less familiar with.

Big deck.

A hundred slides?
Crazy.
Way too many.
Just thinking about our customers.
So we have had the fortune of landing a lot of amazing logos, as you can see here.
This here is where at Homebase I was leading growth previous to this.
And so that's where I signed up to be the first customer and actually cool to see that they are still a customer of ours post that transition.
And so, you know, we've really been able to just find this product market fit where there's all this demand and need for a lot of what we do.
It's been quite exciting for us to be able to land a lot of these great logos.
One of the comments.
One of the things they all have in common is building these organic engines with the right strategy, with the right people, figuring out what's working, what's not.
All of that takes a lot of time, attention, and is quite costly.
You add to the fact that the average tenure of someone who is leading a marketing department is about 14 months, I think, is the average or something to that effect.
That's like a ton of pressure, right?
Like you have to come on, diagnose what the team needs, you know, make sure you're bringing on the right folks, get them coordinated on the right things.
And then you probably have a few bets to get into market before, you know, questions start being asked of you.
And so the need to move fast is a very, very real thing for these businesses.
On top of that...
You have a lot of these, a vast majority of them, if not all, are venture-backed companies.
They've taken VC dollars, and with that comes, that is part of what adds that pressure to grow fast and show results.
One of the ways a lot of these businesses have done that is go after channels such as paid search.
So this is where you can bid on different keywords and, you know, drive people to very targeted landing pages, drive conversions.
And sometimes this is called demand capture, where there is an active intent out there.
People are searching for something.
You are putting out an ad in front of them, capturing that demand.
And that is something that allows these businesses to find some repeatability and grow fast.
The challenge is, if you become too reliant on that single channel, things that are out of your control can add risk to your business.
What if Google makes some big change?
A lot of changes happening, right, in the search space that can put, you know, some of that revenue growth at risk.
Additionally, there's just natural, like, there's going to be diminishing returns as you continue to invest in a channel like that.
And so a lot of these businesses have been kind of, like, stuck in that pattern in different ways, and to varying degrees.
Some have already gone beyond that and are trying to continue to add to their channel mix.
But it's a really, really common challenge, and one that I think sometimes, you know, the team isn't always aware of.
And so I just like to set the stage because that helps just get into the mindset of, like, where the vast majority of our customers are and what they're thinking about.
So with that becomes, okay, how do you lean into organic channels?
How do you start to, you know, grow, say, SEO, show up more in AI Answers?

Workers, et cetera, organic, social, because if you can start to create some repeatability within that, there's a lot more headroom, and it can be really, really cost-effective once you start to figure it out.
The challenge is in taking it to Homebase, like when I joined there, this was a classic case of what I just described, and they were kind of struggling to build that organic engine.
They threw a lot of money into it.
They were getting, like, somewhat okay results, but it was not really scaling, and so that was a big reason they hired me was to come home and help them solve that problem.
And so one of the ways I went about that was hired or just coordinated around, like, who are the freelancers that we have now?
How do I bring in better ones that are going to put out higher-quality content?
I also had an internal team.
I had a web team that was, you know, had a variety of different challenges and capacity in getting things live.
I had...
Limited opportunity to create things like imagery that would support those articles.
We actually were able to find a lot of leverage and move fast within those constraints, but it was really, really expensive.
And also I had to work on a whole variety of other things outside of just doing that.

And so that is like the reality for like a lot of our customers is that they've either probably had some version of that at, those are who are the best fit for us that should qualify, have gone through some version of that at their current role or have seen that in the past role.

And so, you know, just that speed and that challenge of coordinating all those things, and then you get that in the market and then you come back to the average 10-year VSEMO that I mentioned earlier.
It's just a lot of pressure to, you may, you know, it may take you to coordinate some of those things a quarter or so.
Then you get that in the market.
That's another quarter to measure if it works.
And then you have very limited time to adapt and make bets beyond that.
Now, when you bring in AI workflows into the mix in our offering, we can help them move a lot faster in getting this stuff live.
The cost that it takes to get this stuff live is very different.
So for anyone who has kind of experienced that in some meaningful way, understands the value, it becomes a bit of a no-brainer of what we're offering.
That end-to-end of, like, helping create the strategy, helping execute on the content, getting signals back faster, and starting to scale that out, like, that is a real meaningful.
While we are, you know, expensive versus the average kind of content-driven agency, when you actually break down that cost, it's kind of a no-brainer for, like, the deal you're getting.
You know, we're helping address a lot of those key friction points I was mentioning, like, you know, imagery, staging and publishing content, etc.
Like, all that, or...
are meaningful things to address.
If we can do that and execute on the right things, we can help them grow faster.
And ultimately, they can grow faster and they can show results.
That just puts them in a far better spot to starting to diversify their channels within all the constraints I was mentioning.
So important to keep all that in mind because I think at times it's easy to, like, look at some of these numbers and just think about, like, oh, of course we want to grow them and this and that.

But, like, having the context of, like, what is that ultimately, like, trying to, they're trying to really, like, figure out how can they drive more revenue through these channels and drive those sorts of outcomes and how can our content be a piece of that journey that develops that engine for them.
Any questions on that so far?
Cool.
With that in mind, let me find where this slide is.
There's a variety of different places we have experimented with where
So approach of leveraging AI and humans can be applied into developing that organic growth engine.
You know, what we have found over time, though, is that our customers that really start on this side of the house with editorial content are those that are the best fit for us.
What editorial, think of these top two are like really like the two primary outputs of content you'll see us do.
Not universally true, but the vast majority of our accounts will do one of these two, if not both.
And that being editorial content and programmatic content.
And so define those so you have a sense of what we're meaning there.
So think of editorial content as more like longer form content.
This can be things like blogs or articles or just longer form content.
So of
That needs a more needs that more editorial, deeper editorial process and oversight where programmatic content.
This is things that are typically more template based at times could be longer form, shorter form.
Typically, you're going after some sort of set of different variants.
You know, maybe it is, you know, you're setting some sort of like landing page targeting varying industries.
That template is largely the same across those pages.
And then you're scaling up targeting all those different industries.
I'll show you some specific examples just to make sure you're comfortable with it.
But what we've found, though, is like those that if we can start with editorial content and, you know, this could be creating that new content.
This could be like expanding and refreshing their existing content.
It just really is a good place for us to lay the right foundation that we can build upon because no matter.
Or like what stage the brand is at almost every brand we've talked to is lacking in something in their process here.
Some may sometimes come to us and be like thinking they have some stuff figured out and that may still be true to some degree.
But when they get to see the depth of our process and how we think about it, you know, it tends to be this, oh, okay, now this is a bit of like a light bulb moment.
I can see where the value is and how this extends beyond what we do.
And for us, when we talk about laying that foundation, it's things like at the end of the day, what we're trying to do is build something towards who are their ICP, their ideal personas.
What are the different things that motivate those people?
What are their challenges, opportunities, et cetera?
They're naturally going to have a set of different questions associated to those.
And the best we can do to create content that's really.
Meaningful, amazing answers to those questions that helps lay a really healthy foundation for all sorts of channels.
It can be, can help with SEO, it can help with AI answers, can leverage that content even beyond, maybe that is in their email campaigns, whatever it may be.

Because at the end of the day, if you're creating, if you're understanding who their customers are at a deep level and creating something that's truly meaningful for them, you're creating valuable content and content is, is critical for so many aspects of marketing.
And so that's ultimately what we're trying to build towards.
And when we do that for editorial content, you know, we're, we're creating a lot of different artifacts to try to understand.
And maybe you've already seen some of this in our process, but understand kind of what really, you know, understand their products at a deep level, understand who their products are built for.
Again, what motivates those people?
As we start to develop content for them, we're going through a deep calibration process of how does this all take shape within the strategy we're developing and talking to these people?
What's the right tone and voice and structure and things of that nature?
And then as we get this out into market and we get a lot of signals back, you know, which one of these topics that we're prioritizing is actually developing traction?
Why are we developing traction?
Are we not developing traction for some reason?
Just all the different, like, learnings that we get through that can help us continue to iterate within this specifically, but also can open up other paths for us.
And so when we say, like, from content to distribution to conversion, that's kind of what we're referring to here is first you start by developing that content.
You get a lot of those rich signals back.
As you get more of those signals back, that should open up opportunities.
How do we further engage this audience in different ways?
And so, you know, that could be where things like newsletter...
We're just doing other custom work that could be applicable to them in some meaningful way.
We've done things around microsites or, you know, repurposing their content in variety different ways.
We've dabbled with organic social here and there for some of our customers.
And so, you know, those are just different ways you can kind of broaden the reach of that content, continue to engage folks, and then just start to layer in different paths of conversion.
I would say in terms of, like, those other channels, organic social newsletters, it's not like we won't do that.
It's just that's not, like, our core area of focus today.
So, you know, really our specialty is in developing that content.
And as much as any time we've kind of, like, broadened outside of that, at least today, taken on really custom work, it's pretty rare we don't end up regretting that in some way.
So we're trying to come back to, like, who is our ICP?
Continuing to learn about those different traits, who are the right people, and process, and all that goes into making that as repeatable as possible.
And so we've just found the more and more we lean into that editorial layer, we build our process, our workflows, all that into that.
It just, everything feels a lot more repeatable.
So it's not that we won't do anything outside of we're just going to be really selective.
And we just continue to be more and more selective to just make sure we set up the team and everyone else up for success.
I can jump in just an example of what programmatic content looks like, because that is something, you know, we'll do more and more.
You may certainly be involved in some accounts that do this to some degree.
But before I jump into that, any questions?
Yeah, I got a quick question.
I know Marcel has talked a lot about how retention is like a big goal for the company from those original logos.
That you showed in the beginning, like how many of those clients have been with GrowthX, AI, since the beginning?
Have you managed to retain?
Since the beginning?
I mean, a lot of, a healthy amount of these are new-ish.
But there's some on here who have been here quite a while.
Our retention has been quite strong recently.
We had some, we had a bit of, did anyone talk to you guys about our, like, strategy sprint process yet?
Or I don't want to be duplicative here, but that's somewhat related to this point.
No, not in my case.
No, I haven't heard that yet.
Okay.
I think there's a slide in here somewhere.

But related to this question, you know, where some of our churn came from, it was certainly heavier earlier in the year, and there was just a lot of learnings related to kind of what I've been talking about.
I don't

Maybe they got rid of the slide, but point being is essentially the quick on our journey of how we used to handle contracts is we used to do them relatively short on these rolling three-month engagements.
That made sense in the early lifecycle of our company just to get buy-in from companies because we were so new.
Then we started moving to 12-month agreements because, you know, that just helps show momentum in our business in a whole variety of ways.
It's appealing to investors, things of that nature.
And how we did that was initially introducing this 90-day pilot at the beginning of that 12-month engagement.
And that was just a – that provided companies a way for getting in the door, seeing if this is a fit for them, et cetera.
But that's, like, really short window of time to show results from SEO and editorial content.
And additionally, we were asking for 30 days' notice.
was really
We're like 60 days and that's just like not really the most ideal way of thinking about it.
So we actually did a pretty aggressive pivot back in May of this year where we introduced the concept of a strategy sprint.
And so a strategy sprint essentially being like, you know, it's an eight week process.
It can still opt out at the end of that, but the structure and the mindset of it, we can also opt out.
It's about really trying to lay the right foundation for strategy, scope the right agreement for a longer term contract after that.
And so it's built into a single SoW.
They can opt out at the end of that.
We've only had one opt out so far, and that was one that was like probably not the greatest fit for us.

And so what we're doing is we're laying a lot of the foundation of strategy, developing all those different artifacts, executing against that, calibrating on it, and then more deeply appreciating, like, what do they need to be successful repeatedly?
And so that's...

That's been a really key change for us, and the reason that kind of ties to churn is just really making sure that we are early on not just jumping in and executing as fast as possible, because speed is a bit of a superpower of ours, as I was mentioning earlier, but making sure we're executing on the right things meaningfully and really aligning with them on, like, this is what we are executing on and why.
And so if we do that in a healthy way, that should ultimately lead to less churn is the hypothesis.
And so if you look at some of these, like, a lot of these are still here.
Reddit is one that churned.

That was, you know, a great logo for us for a while, but, you know, ultimately, like, they had some just, like, real challenges with how that team operated that just were tough for us to navigate around.
And a few others in here that, like, were opt-outs during the sprint, or the, not the sprint, the old model.
Yeah.
So, yeah.
I
But ultimately, since we've made this change, our turn has been very, very healthy.
We're in a great spot from that standpoint.
We do have a lot of renewals coming up, and so that's going to be a really key period of time for us to show further momentum.
But largely, we're feeling good and optimistic, but certainly more work to be done.
And so coming back to focusing on the right use cases, that's just been really, really key for us.
Knowing we just have a few minutes, let me just share one quick example of programmatic, because I find that, like, it's not uncommon for folks to join here and know what programmatic is.
But I'd say more often than not, when I'm on these calls, people aren't as familiar with programmatic, SEO, or content.
So this is an example for Ramp, one of our oldest customers.
There was an opportunity we found back in the fall.
We've done all sorts of programmatic things for them.
And I hope so.
This is one of the early ones we executed on.
We found that, you know, there was a lot of search volume around variance, around per diem rates.
And so what that means is, like, as you're doing business travel, there's varying per diem for, like, allowances of expensing your meals and things of that nature for different cities and states.
And Ramp actually has a solution for this.
This is, like, a feature of their product to help manage that for teams.
And so we found that there was a lot of, again, search volume for this, a lot of different variance.

And this is where programmatic can be effective is, you know, sometimes you'll hear this called, like, the head turn, you know, where this is, like, the theme, people searching for different per diem rates.
And so there's people who are searching for per diem California, per diem San Francisco, et cetera.
And we found that there was just, like, you
A lot of search volume, the difficulty wasn't super high, the user experiences of the content that was ranking was pretty bad.
So what we developed was this template for them.
Again, this being a repeatable template, a lot of these H1s, things of that nature, will be quite similar across them, but the variant will be either the state or the city.
That's true of other places of this page.
This is unique content to this page.
These are the actual per diem rates in California by county.
And so this was things that we could scrape from different government websites.
And so programmatic has been around for a long time.
I've done a lot of this in pre-AI days.
What's different, though, is with what is possible now with AI, we can scale and do things a lot faster.
And so, you know, this isn't going to be a fit for every customer of ours, but there are some that are going to just have like...
This is of programmatic opportunities.
Oftentimes what we need to make sure is like when is the right time to do something like this.
It's not about just blasting a lot of content for the sake of blasting content like you want to be.
Make sure, again, you're creating a good, valuable user experience.

In this case, because we were seeing what was out there and how bad the user experiences were and the relative low difficulty of ranking for these things, was kind of like, okay, this is an easy win to put out there.
So we scaled out all these different pages and kind of brought them together.
So for some of our customers, this can be an awesome thing to do.
The difference here is like, you know, back to like, you know, we had the editorial bucket, we had the programmatic bucket.

Like when this becomes more repeatable, it's not like we're not reviewing this content, but the level of like reviewing and editing a template like this is not going to be the same level as like a blog that you're doing each and every time, right?
Like a blog in a sense, it's going to feel more bespoke.
Like a in a

Yes, there's repeatability we'll create in that in varying ways, but, you know, this tends to oftentimes take more time to get off the ground, set up the template, set up the strategy, all this and that.
But once you get it going, you can really broaden the reach and volume of something like this.

But far more what we prefer to do is, like, a good example of, like, what we want to do the most of is something like an augment code where, you know, if you go to their slash guides page, like, all of this is our content that we've done with them since, I'm trying to remember, I think it was July or something like that.
Yeah, it looks like it was about mid-July we first started with them.
And so this is all the content we've done since, which is, like, pretty nuts.
And so this is all just, like, editorial-based content, given they're, like, one of our highest-paying customers, so we're going to put out a lot more content than the average.
But this is one of the success stories for sure.
Like, how fast we can develop growth for a client.
Like, I'll just pull it real quick.
Does that kind of programmatic content require, like, a lot more artifacts to upload onto Atlas to make sure that the information is correct?
Or does it require a lot more research, cetera, like, more than a regular editorial client?
Not necessarily more artifacts, although there could be situations where it may necessitate multiple artifacts.
Maybe it is, like, you know, going after needing to deepen, like, talking about a particular persona.
But because of the templatized nature of it, it should be something that doesn't require as all sorts of variants of artifacts to make repeatable.
Really, it comes more down to, like, customizing the workflows to make it that work, right?
Like, so think of it as, like, thanks, Gloria, take care.
Think of it as, like.

Okay, like, you know, the headers here, and then there's a paragraph here, and what is that, and like, each programmatic opportunity you're setting up is going to be different, and so you, we would need to, like, customize the workflows to represent that.

There oftentimes can be, like, a database component to this, in some ways, I will go to in the weeds on that, but essentially, like, you know, that's where it becomes a little bit more complex to get live early on, but it's very doable, it's just a bit, the process to do that versus create an article, which is quite different.
Some of the research, though, could be similar, right?
It's, you know, again, who are these people, what do they care about, what are they searching for, but it's just a bit of, like, you know, what is that right experience to create for them?
And so it's always good to kind of, like, think about that, right?
It's, like, we shouldn't just always assume an article in a blog is going to be the best thing.
For these people, right?
It's like, who are these people?
What is the intent behind this?
How can we create something really meaningful?
Anytime I've been able to find whatever that right recipe is for brands I've worked with or worked at, that just tends to work quite well.

And so what I've seen is when you just blindly, I've seen brands who would just blindly copy other brands without thinking about that intent and thinking about that, it's going to be unique to each brand and industry and persona.
When you can find something that is truly meaningful, that's what's going to typically work.
You can draw inspiration, but if you're just blindly copy counting, that's, I've just sometimes had customers come to us and like, look at this brand, love what they did here.
It's like, yeah, but that's like, not at all applicable to what you particularly are and do.
But anyways, hopefully some of this was helpful for y'all.
No doubt, I'm sure y'all have questions as you continue to get up to speed.
Just given the context I mentioned earlier of just like all
Different sides have been able to see with GrowthX, just keep that in mind, happy to answer any questions for y'all, to find some time to go deeper in a particular area, just to let me know.
Great.

I love it.

So that's like a, because his nickname's Larry Legend, it's kind of a fun little play.

Okay.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Cool.

It's my first date.
So I have been really good.
Thank you.
I haven't gotten through a lot.
Yeah.
Starting up.
And, yeah, if I have questions, will surely keep.
Sounds great.
Well, super excited.
You both are here.
Yeah.
Just let me know if I can be of help in any way.
Will do.
Thank you so much for your time.
All right.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Onboard new team members by providing an overview of GrowthX.ai's customer use cases and business context.](https://fathom.video/share/jsDqa2_GhrUx6SsYxoTfXmgZjTcxNxGD?tab=summary&timestamp=170.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [GrowthX.ai helps venture-backed companies diversify from paid channels to organic growth, addressing pain points like high costs and slow execution](https://fathom.video/share/jsDqa2_GhrUx6SsYxoTfXmgZjTcxNxGD?tab=summary&timestamp=418.0)

## Topics

### Customer Context and Pain Points

  - [Often over-reliant on paid channels (e.g. paid search), seeking to diversify to organic](https://fathom.video/share/jsDqa2_GhrUx6SsYxoTfXmgZjTcxNxGD?tab=summary&timestamp=435.0)
  - [Building organic engines is time-consuming, costly, and challenging to coordinate](https://fathom.video/share/jsDqa2_GhrUx6SsYxoTfXmgZjTcxNxGD?tab=summary&timestamp=554.0)
  - [Average CMO tenure of 14 months creates urgency to show results](https://fathom.video/share/jsDqa2_GhrUx6SsYxoTfXmgZjTcxNxGD?tab=summary&timestamp=379.0)
